questions.question: What is the core principle of group music and movement facilitation?

options.text: Demonstrate advanced skills and expect participants to follow perfectly
Create a safe, inclusive container where participants feel empowered to engage at their own level—facilitator guides the process, not performs
Focus only on the most advanced or skilled participants
Avoid structure and let chaos guide the session

questions.explanation: Group facilitation is about creating safety, inclusion, and empowerment. The facilitator's role is to guide the process (hold the container) rather than perform or demonstrate mastery. Participants engage at their own level (no forced participation, modifications welcome). The focus is on the group's collective experience and safety, not the facilitator's skills or advanced participants' performance.

questions.question: Why is informed consent important before group music and movement sessions?

options.text: It's not important—just start the session
Participants need to know what will happen, what's expected (nothing is mandatory), and that they can opt out at any time—this ensures psychological safety and prevents harm
Only for legal protection
To scare people away

questions.explanation: Informed consent ensures psychological safety. Participants need to know: (1) what will happen in the session (structure, activities), (2) nothing is mandatory (they can observe, modify, or opt out), (3) they can leave at any time without judgment. This prevents surprises that could trigger trauma responses and empowers participants to make informed choices about their engagement.

questions.question: How should a facilitator respond when a participant becomes visibly distressed during a group session?

options.text: Ignore it and continue the session—they'll be fine
Stop the entire group and focus all attention on the distressed person
Pause, check in privately if possible ('Are you okay? Do you need a break?'), offer options (leave, observe, ground), have a support person available, and adjust group intensity if needed
Tell them they're overreacting

questions.explanation: When someone shows distress: (1) pause and check in (privately if possible to avoid public spotlight), (2) offer options (leave, take a break, observe, grounding techniques), (3) don't force them to continue or explain, (4) have a support person available for private support, (5) assess if group intensity needs adjustment. The goal is to support the individual without creating shame or disrupting group safety.

questions.question: What are the three essential phases of a group music and movement session?

options.text: Performance, critique, and dismissal
Opening/grounding (safety, consent, orienting), Main practice (core activity with pacing and choices), Closing/integration (grounding, reflection, transition back)
Warm-up, competition, and cool-down
Lecture, demonstration, and testing

questions.explanation: Every group session needs: (1) Opening/grounding—establish safety, explain structure, get consent, orient to space and group, (2) Main practice—core activity (drumming, singing, dance) with appropriate pacing, modification options, and facilitator guidance, (3) Closing/integration—grounding practice, reflection/sharing circle (optional), explicit transition back to daily life. These phases create psychological safety and prevent leaving participants activated or destabilized.

questions.question: When should you refer a participant to professional mental health services rather than continuing group music and movement facilitation?

options.text: Never—group facilitation can handle all mental health needs
When they express suicidal thoughts, severe dissociation, psychosis, or symptoms beyond wellness support scope—group facilitation is not therapy and cannot replace clinical care
When they disagree with your facilitation style
Only if they ask for a referral

questions.explanation: Group facilitation is for wellness, stress reduction, and community support—NOT clinical treatment. Refer to professionals when participants show: suicidal thoughts/self-harm, severe dissociation or flashbacks, psychosis, trauma symptoms beyond your training, substance use crisis, or other clinical mental health conditions. Group facilitators hold the container for wellness; therapists provide clinical treatment. Know your scope and boundaries.
